g.data-narration: Introduction to Operating System Structure - understanding OS architecture models for CSE syllabus.
An OS is complex software. Partitioning into modules makes it maintainable - structure defines component organization.
g.text: An OS is a large, complex software. Partitioning into smaller, well-defined
components (modules) makes it maintainable. The interconnection and organization
of these components defines the OS structure.

g.data-narration: Five main OS architecture models: Monolithic, Layered, Microkernel, Modular, and Hybrid systems.
Monolithic OS has all functionality in kernel space - MS-DOS is a simple example with flat structure.
Early UNIX also used monolithic design with system call interface between applications and kernel services.
g.text: Kernel: Signals, File System, Memory, CPU Scheduling

g.data-narration: Monolithic kernels offer high performance but are hard to debug and maintain - single failure crashes system.
THE system pioneered layered approach - each layer only uses services from layers below it.
Layered systems provide easy debugging through abstraction but defining correct layer order is challenging.
g.text: Difficult to define correct layer order

g.data-narration: Microkernel keeps only essential functions in kernel - IPC, memory management, and CPU scheduling.
g.text: Only essential functions in kernel; others run as user-space services

g.data-narration: Popular microkernels include Mach (used in macOS), MINIX (educational), QNX (real-time), and L4 (high-performance).
Microkernels are reliable and secure but suffer from slow IPC due to message passing overhead.
g.text: Reliable (service crash does not crash system)

g.data-narration: Linux uses modular approach with loadable kernel modules that can be dynamically loaded and unloaded at runtime.
g.text: Core kernel plus dynamically loadable modules (run in kernel space)

g.data-narration: Linux provides insmod, rmmod, lsmod, and modprobe commands to manage loadable kernel modules.
Modern OS use hybrid architecture - macOS combines Mach and BSD, Windows uses layered kernel, Android adds framework to Linux.
Comparison summary shows monolithic has best performance but lowest reliability, while microkernel offers highest reliability and extensibility.
